comment_text.sn4.16:3.2: The ox was a metaphor for the senses, which are “yoked” like an ox by desire (SN 35.232:4.1). The relation between Māra, sense control, and oxen is emphasized in SN 4.19.
comment_text.sn4.16:3.4: The bowl is a symbol of mendicant life. Within it is the food that sustains life, but which also tempts desire. The rampant ox is unrestrained desire, which threatens to crack open the bounds of monastic life.
comment_text.sn4.16:4.2: These are the five aggregates. To fit the metre, vedayitaṁ (“the felt”) is used for vedanā (“feeling”), and saṅkhataṁ “the chosen”) is used for saṅkhārā (“choices”).

root_text.sn4.16:1.2: Tena kho pana samayena bhagavā pañcannaṁ upādānakkhandhānaṁ upādāya bhikkhūnaṁ dhammiyā kathāya sandasseti samādapeti samuttejeti sampahaṁseti.
root_text.sn4.16:1.3: Te ca bhikkhū aṭṭhiṁ katvā manasi katvā sabbacetasā samannāharitvā ohitasotā dhammaṁ suṇanti.
root_text.sn4.16:2.2: “ayaṁ kho samaṇo gotamo pañcannaṁ upādānakkhandhānaṁ upādāya bhikkhūnaṁ dhammiyā kathāya sandasseti samādapeti samuttejeti sampahaṁseti.
root_text.sn4.16:2.3: Te ca bhikkhū aṭṭhiṁ katvā manasi katvā sabbacetasā samannāharitvā ohitasotā dhammaṁ suṇanti.
root_text.sn4.16:2.4: Yannūnāhaṁ yena samaṇo gotamo tenupasaṅkameyyaṁ vicakkhukammāyā”ti.
root_text.sn4.16:3.1: Tena kho pana samayena sambahulā pattā abbhokāse nikkhittā honti.
root_text.sn4.16:3.2: Atha kho māro pāpimā balībaddavaṇṇaṁ abhinimminitvā yena te pattā tenupasaṅkami.
root_text.sn4.16:3.3: Atha kho aññataro bhikkhu aññataraṁ bhikkhuṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn4.16:3.5: Evaṁ vutte, bhagavā taṁ bhikkhuṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn4.16:3.7: Māro eso pāpimā tumhākaṁ vicakkhukammāya āgato”ti.
root_text.sn4.16:3.8: Atha kho bhagavā “māro ayaṁ pāpimā” iti viditvā māraṁ pāpimantaṁ gāthāya ajjhabhāsi:

translation_text.sn4.16:1.2: Now at that time the Buddha was educating, encouraging, firing up, and inspiring the mendicants with a Dhamma talk on the topic of the five grasping aggregates.
translation_text.sn4.16:1.3: And those mendicants were paying attention, applying the mind, concentrating wholeheartedly, and actively listening.
translation_text.sn4.16:2.2: “This ascetic Gotama is educating, encouraging, firing up, and inspiring the mendicants with a Dhamma talk on the topic of the five grasping aggregates.
translation_text.sn4.16:2.3: And the mendicants are paying attention, applying the mind, concentrating wholeheartedly, and actively listening.
translation_text.sn4.16:2.4: Why don’t I go and pull the wool over their eyes?”
translation_text.sn4.16:3.1: At that time several alms bowls were placed in the open air.
translation_text.sn4.16:3.2: Then Māra the Wicked manifested in the form of an ox and approached those bowls.
translation_text.sn4.16:3.3: One of the mendicants said to another,
translation_text.sn4.16:3.4: “Mendicant, mendicant, that ox will break the bowls.”
translation_text.sn4.16:3.5: When this was said, the Buddha said to that mendicant,
translation_text.sn4.16:3.7: That’s Māra the Wicked come to pull the wool over your eyes!”
translation_text.sn4.16:3.8: Then the Buddha, knowing that this was Māra the Wicked, addressed him in verse:
translation_text.sn4.16:4.3: ‘I am not this’ and ‘this is not mine’;
translation_text.sn4.16:4.4: that’s how to be free of desire for them.
translation_text.sn4.16:5.3: though Māra and his army chase everywhere

variant_text.sn4.16:1.3: aṭṭhiṁ katvā → aṭṭhikatvā (bj, sya-all, km, pts1ed, pts2ed) | sabbacetasā → sabbacetaso (bj, sya-all, km, pts1ed); sabbaṁ cetasā (mr)

reference_text.sn4.16:1.1: csp1ed12.114, csp2ed12.114, dr15.155, ms12S1_847, msdiv152, pts-vp-pli1ed1.112
